length_bias.note: 1 of 6 MCQs (q2) has correct answer exceeding 1.20x distractor mean. Rate 0.167 <= 0.40 threshold. q1/q5/q6 all cleared after cycle-2 distractor expansion. q2 marginal overshoot (110 vs threshold 105.6) acceptable within the rate budget.

scoring_summary.note: All distractors are plausible misconceptions: language-rules confusion, flowchart confusion, code-compression confusion.
Python syntax distractor is a strong 4; vague-description and flowchart distractors are solid 3s.

Abstraction and decomposition are plausible adjacent-concept confusions (3). 'Decision and compilation' scores 2 — compilation is a heard-but-misunderstood term, plausible but not a canonical building-block misconception.
All three distractors map to identifiable trace-order errors beginners make.
Reversed-description distractor is a strong 4; step-limit and indentation distractors are plausible 3s.
Bug-guarantee misconception scores 4; language-requirement misconception scores 3; internet-search rationale scores 2 (not a canonical misconception but not absurd).

weakest_questions.note: Soft finding: 'compilation' is not taught in this topic; used only as a wrong-answer foil. Students can eliminate it without knowing what compilation means. Does not cross into absurd territory.

weakest_questions.distractor: It makes it easier to copy relevant code from the internet because you already know exactly what logic to search for, and it lets you avoid learning any real programming language entirely.
weakest_questions.note: Soft finding: not a canonical beginner misconception about pseudocode; slightly implausible as a genuine held belief. Remains above absurd (score=1) threshold.

coverage_map.text: Explain in your own words what pseudocode is and why it exists.
Write basic pseudocode that describes a simple, real-world process using plain English.
Identify the core building blocks of pseudocode: sequence, decision (IF/OTHERWISE), and repetition (REPEAT/WHILE).
Translate a short block of pseudocode into the equivalent steps you would follow manually.
Recognise the difference between pseudocode and actual programming code.
Describe at least two reasons why writing pseudocode before coding helps you think more clearly.

beginner_fairness_scope.note: All stems use plain language defined in the corpus. References to 'abstraction' and 'decomposition' in q3 distractors are grounded in corpus §2 (prerequisites). The Python indentation fact in q5 is explicitly covered in corpus §5.5. No stem or correct answer requires knowledge outside this topic.

verdict: Pass — all checks clean in cycle 2. Length bias resolved: q1/q5/q6 distractors expanded; longest_correct_rate dropped from 0.50 to 0.167 (below 0.40 threshold). Positional bias, distractor plausibility, objective coverage, and beginner-fairness all pass.
